summary: base58-core ships a crypto-clipper, credential stealer, and persistence backdoor disguised as a base58 encoder
details: Despite advertising itself as a zero-dependency base58 encoder, base58-core@1.0.8 executes a wallet-theft and credential-harvest payload on require. After a 72-hour dormancy window (to evade CI and sandbox analysis), dist/index.cjs and dist/index.js poll the clipboard every 2.5 seconds and substitute any BTC, ETH, or SOL address with hardcoded attacker addresses (bc1qjft978uykglsh0adcyx6xhkes56vqzs3fual3l, 0xd63eD44065eDb1e2ad2519B011c06412dA7B7c5B, A7ajd7W5WYdrnkeaiBRjVoK6uBEDvgnuZcpzQXqo18Ph), redirecting any transaction the installer pastes to the attacker. When wallet-shaped strings (WIF/hex private keys, BIP39 seed phrases) appear in the clipboard, the original content plus hostname/platform/homedir/cwd is POSTed to a hardcoded plain-HTTP endpoint at 2.27.62.51:8080/api/health (with:8081 as backup). A reconnaissance routine enumerates the MetaMask extension storage (nkbihfbeogaeaoehlefnkodbefgpgknn), Telegram Desktop tdata, Chromium-family browser profiles, ~/.ssh keys and config, ~/.npmrc _authToken, ~/.env files, and crypto-related environment variables (MNEMONIC, PRIVATE_KEY, SEED_PHRASE), shipping results to the same attacker IP. Persistence is established by appending a node loader hook to ~/.bashrc, ~/.zshrc, and ~/.profile, and by dropping base58-runtime.js into the Windows Startup folder, so the payload re-activates in every new shell or login session. The package name and README banner ('@base58/core') impersonate the legitimate base58 namespace to maximize installs.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
